intro: Combined events involve two or more actions happening together or in sequence. Sample space diagrams and the multiplication rule help you find the probability of combined outcomes.
explanation:
Sample space diagrams:
A table showing all possible outcomes of two events.
Example: Rolling two dice. Draw a grid with values 1-6 on each axis and list all 36 possible outcome pairs.

Independent events:
Two events are independent if the outcome of one does not affect the other.
P(A and B) = P(A) x P(B).
Example: P(heads) x P(roll a 6) = 1/2 x 1/6 = 1/12.

P(A or B) for non-mutually exclusive events:
P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B).

Ordered outcomes:
Sometimes order matters — use a sample space diagram or systematic list.
Example: Two coins. Sample space: HH, HT, TH, TT. P(at least one head) = 3/4.

Conditional hint for combined events:
When events are NOT independent (without replacement), the probability of the second event depends on the first. Use tree diagrams for these (see Tree Diagrams topic).

worked_example.question: Two fair dice are rolled. Using a sample space diagram, find: (a) P(sum = 7)  (b) P(both show the same number)  (c) P(at least one 4)
steps: (c) At least one 4: 6 with first die + 6 with second die - 1 (both 4) = 11. P = 11/36.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to subtract overlapping outcomes in P(A or B) for non-mutually exclusive events — failing to subtract P(A and B) leads to double-counting.
Counting the sample space incorrectly — draw the full grid or list to be sure of the total number of outcomes.
Applying P(A and B) = P(A) x P(B) to events that are not independent — only use the multiplication rule when events are independent.
